other_observers: Doug Faulkner, Bill & Jim Schmoker, Dick Schottler, Peter Gent, Ric Olson, Rachel Hopper, Larry Semo, Loch Kilpatrick, Joey Kellner, and others; Mike Henwood found the bird on the morning of 8 November. He called me about 1335 MST and I got everyone else going.
description: The bird was an obvious small alcid (much smaller than Gadwalls -- about half the length and only, at most, 1/4 the bulk -- and Com. Goldeneyes -- about 2/3 length -- in direct comparison; overall dark above, white below, with a very roundish shape). The head was mostly black, except for white chin, throat, and an intrusion of white from the throat up and around the auriculars. The upperparts were all medium gray. The sides were whitish mottled with medium gray and the undertail coverts, as well as the rest of the underparts, were white. The bill was pale, sometimes appearing yellowish, but others looking grayish or horn-colored - depending on the light.  I saw the bird fly a couple times and it did so with very rapid, shallow wing beats. At these times, the outer half of the wing looked dark and contrasted somewhat with the paler inner half. The wing linings appeared to be white, though, due to the shallow and rapid wing beats, it was difficult to be sure. I believe that the remiges were dark from underneath.  When the bird landed after its "long" flight covering half the pond, it made a spectacular three-bounce landing, typical of small alcids.Behaviors: The bird, for the most part, idled on the water's surface. However, as I got there before most birders, I saw it dive on apparent foraging trips at least four times. When it dove, the bird would open its wings and "fly" down under the surface. I don't know that it captured anything on any dives, as, if it did, the items must have been small, because I could not discern anything in the bill upon its bobbing to the surface.  At a couple of points when it was in the SW corner of the pond near Common Goldeneyes, it threatened a nearby goldeneye with a bird-typical thrusting forward of the head and gaping of the bill.Call: none heardPlumage: juvenile
